content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

We shipped a frontend update on a Thursday. Everything passed — unit tests, integration tests, staging QA. Lighthouse scores actually went up. We were feeling pretty good about it.
Monday morning, support forwarded an email from a customer. Attached was a screenshot of our pricing page showing every plan at $0/mo. Not a billing bug — a JavaScript error in the price rendering component that only triggered with a specific locale setting. The page loaded fine, returned 200, looked perfect on our machines.
The prices had been showing $0 for three days. Over a weekend. On the one page that matters most.
Why tests didn’t catch it
Our test suite checked that price elements existed in the DOM. They did. It checked that the API returned correct values. It did. What nobody tested was whether the rendered output — the thing a human actually sees — matched what we expected.
This is the gap between functional testing and visual testing, and it’s wider than most teams realize.
Functional tests answer “does the code work?” Visual tests answer “does the page look right?” Those are very different questions. A button can pass every functional test while being completely hidden behind another element. A table can render correctly in Chrome and break in Safari. An image can load on staging and 404 in production because the CDN path differs.
What we changed after that
We added automated screenshots to our CI pipeline. Not the complicated kind — no AI-powered visual recognition or pixel-perfect comparison engines. Just: take a screenshot of these 12 pages after every deploy, diff them against the previous version, flag anything that changed by more than a threshold.
First week, it caught three things we would’ve missed:
A font that fell back to Times New Roman because the Google Fonts import got removed during a CSS refactor. Nobody noticed in code review.
A “Sign Up Free” button that shifted 40px down on mobile because someone added a banner above it. Still visible, still clickable, but now below the fold on shorter screens.
A footer that straight up disappeared on one page. A conditional render that evaluated wrong after a state management change. Page still loaded, tests still passed.
The part nobody talks about
The hardest thing about visual testing isn’t the tooling. It’s deciding what counts as a meaningful change.
You take screenshots every deploy, you’re going to see diffs constantly. A date changes, an ad rotates, dynamic content shifts things around. If you alert on every pixel change you’ll get paged ten times a day and start ignoring alerts within a week.
What worked for us: separate the pages into tiers. Critical pages (pricing, checkout, login) get tight thresholds — anything over 2% diff triggers a review. Content pages (blog, docs) get looser thresholds or just weekly checks. Marketing pages with dynamic content get excluded from automated diffing entirely.
Start with your three most important pages. Just three. Take before/after screenshots on deploy. That’s it. You can get fancy later.
Screenshots vs snapshot tests
Worth clarifying because people mix these up. Jest snapshot tests compare serialized DOM output — they catch structural changes in your component tree. Screenshot tests capture what the browser actually renders — they catch visual issues that survive DOM comparisons.
A snapshot test wouldn’t have caught our $0 pricing bug. The DOM was correct. The JavaScript that processed the locale and formatted the price was the problem, and it only manifested visually.
Both are useful. They test different layers. Don’t skip one thinking the other covers it.
The boring truth
Visual bugs are boring. Nobody writes post-mortems about a misaligned button or a missing image. They just quietly erode trust. A customer sees a broken layout and doesn’t file a bug report — they just leave. You never hear about it.
Automated screenshots aren’t exciting. Setting them up doesn’t feel like real engineering work. But they catch the stuff that slips through every other safety net, and that makes them worth the thirty minutes it takes to set up.
